title: Type to the board, hear it answer
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Upload a sketch with no wiring, open Serial Monitor, and type. Each character you send crosses the USB cable into a small holding area on the board; the sketch checks how much is waiting, reads a whole line, and answers. Learn how input actually arrives — one byte at a time, waiting to be read — because that is what lets a board stop running a fixed script and start taking commands from you while it runs.

sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

parts.name: Official sketch
parts.explanation: A sketch is the Arduino name for a program. This lesson uses Freenove's Sketch_19.1_Ultrasonic_Ranging.ino so you can focus first on wiring, observation, and the measurement model.

coachInstructions: Keep the working day to upload, type, and read the reply; there is nothing to wire.
Teach the mechanism when asked - typed characters land in the board's receive buffer one byte at a time, available() reports how many are waiting, and read() takes one out.
Explain why the check comes first - read() on an empty buffer returns -1, so available() lets the board wait for real input instead of acting on that -1.
Point out that bytes arrive as character codes (A is 65), which is why readStringUntil gathers a whole line and why toInt() or a comparison is what turns input into a command.

If nothing echoes back, the Newline line ending is the first thing to check — the board waits for it to end a message; if text is garbled, confirm 115200 baud; if the monitor is blank at start, check board and port and press reset.
For the challenge, keep the working read loop and add to it - a word check that switches the onboard LED, then toInt() to act on a typed number; the onboard LED needs no wiring.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython's input() reads from Thonny's shell and is optional only if already set up.
steps: Plug the ESP32-S3 into your computer with a data-capable USB cable.
In Arduino IDE, select your ESP32-S3 board and the port it appears on.
Press Upload and wait for the IDE to say Done uploading.
Open Serial Monitor, set the baud rate to 115200, and set the line ending to Newline.
Type a word into the input box, press Enter, and watch the board echo it back.

arduino.explanation: Reports how many of your characters are sitting in the receive buffer, so the board reads only when something has actually arrived rather than acting on an empty read.
Pulls characters out of the buffer one after another and stops at the Enter key, handing you one whole message instead of loose bytes.
Trims the stray newline and spaces off the ends so the message you act on is exactly what you typed.
Sends your message straight back as proof it was read, then the sketch clears its variable and waits for the next line.

micropython.explanation: In MicroPython, input() waits for a line you type in Thonny (https://thonny.org)'s shell — the same pause-and-read as Serial.available plus readStringUntil.
Echoes your typed line straight back, just like the Arduino sketch.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: Each character you type crosses the same USB wires into a receive buffer; available() counts what's waiting, read() takes it out one byte at a time, and your code decides what it means.
theoryModel.formula: characters land in a buffer → available() counts → read() takes one → your code acts on it

notes.title: Why check available() before you read
notes.body: Calling read() on an empty buffer returns -1, its stand-in for nothing here. Checking available() first means the board waits for real input instead of mistaking that -1 for something you sent.

notes.title: Bytes arrive, not words
notes.body: The board receives raw character codes one at a time, so a five-letter word is five separate bytes. readStringUntil gathers them up to your Enter key into one whole message; a comparison or toInt() then turns that message into something to act on.

test.successCriteria: The start-up lines print once; after that, each line you send comes straight back prefixed with "inputString:".

firstChecks: Set the line ending to Newline so the board sees where your message ends.
Set Serial Monitor to 115200 baud to match the sketch.
Confirm the right board and port, then press the board's reset button.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Add the onboard LED to the sketch, then make the board switch it with the word you type — first as an on/off command, then by acting on a typed number.
